Examine <kanji>reality</kanji> and extract its <kanji>marrow</kanji> — this will let you perceive its <vocabulary>essence</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>soul</vocabulary>. Ponder these things hard enough, and you may even discover the <vocabulary>true meaning</vocabulary> of life, the universe, and everything.

真髄 is a formal word used to describe the true essence or core of something — often with a bit of literary flair. It's used when talking about the deepest meaning or most important part of an idea, skill, or philosophy.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

data.meaning_mnemonic: This radical is the same as the kanji. It means <radical>doubt</radical>.

A <radical>wing</radical> is something you <kanji>must not</kanji> do anything with. Wings are fragile (think bird's wings and butterfly's wings) and sometimes dangerous (like the wings of a plane) and you must not touch them, or even go near them! You MUST NOT. You. MUST. NOT.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine a pair of wings with a prohibition sign (a red circle with a red line) on them. And next time you see a wing of any kind, think "I MUST NOT"!
data.reading_mnemonic: Apart from staying away from wings, the other thing you <kanji>must not</kanji> do is eat <reading>mochi</reading> (もち). Okay, they're not fragile or dangerous, but I love mochi and I don't want you eating them.
data.reading_hint: Now imagine some mochi with red prohibition circles on them. You MUST NOT eat those, do you hear me? Those mochi are mine!

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You've got a combination of <kanji>China</kanji> and 揚げ, meaning "fried." That makes this Chinese-style <vocabulary>Fried Chicken</vocabulary>, or as you'll probably also know it, <vocabulary>karaage</vocabulary>.

While 唐揚げ most often refers to fried chicken, it can technically also refer to other deep fried meats, fish, or vegetables. As it's the most common meaning, remembering this as "fried chicken" will be just fine, though.
data.reading_mnemonic:
You know how to read the 揚げ part, but this uses the kun'yomi reading for 唐, which is new. You'll likely be able to remember this reading by associating fried chicken with Japanese karaage, but in case you're not familiar with it, here's a mnemonic to help you:

To make this kind of <vocabulary>fried chicken</vocabulary>, you have to first give it one strong <reading>から</reading>て chop. If you fry it up without that からて chop, it just comes out terrible no matter what you do. So just remember that next time you make it for me, kay?
